reasoning:
The tech job market, specifically software development, has been in a severe long-term downtrend since its 2022 peak. Current data (as of March 30, 2025) shows the index at 62.55, which is roughly 37.5% below the pre-pandemic baseline, having dropped from ~70.86 a year ago and ~67.45 in January 2025. Although the most recent two weeks showed a modest volatile bounce (climbing from 61.63 up to 62.80 before softening to 62.55), the structural downward momentum remains strong. The FRED tool's linear projection model estimates roughly a 36% chance of the index exceeding the current threshold by April 6, factoring in the prevailing negative slope. Since the data is seasonally adjusted, short-term upswings are less likely to purely reflect routine seasonal hiring. 

We model the short-term (1-week and 1-month) probabilities around 35–38% because the recent negative momentum and historical downward drift are likely to persist, making an increase less probable than a decrease. For the medium and longer-term horizons (3-month, 6-month, and 1-year), the uncertainty naturally grows. The tech sector may eventually hit a cyclical bottom and stabilize. Consequently, the probabilities remain below 50% due to the established multi-year trend, but systematically increase towards 0.5 (representing maximum uncertainty and a higher chance of structural recovery) as the forecast horizon extends through early 2026.
